source: World Bank Open Data + Claude editorial

GBR.text: The United Kingdom spends 18.6% of its budget on healthcare and achieves a life expectancy of 81.2 years with an infant mortality rate of 4 per 1,000 live births, while the United States spends 25.9% of its budget and achieves a life expectancy of 78.4 years with an infant mortality rate of 5.5 per 1,000 live births. The UK provides universal coverage to all residents while the US leaves 27 million people uninsured despite higher spending.

DEU.text: Germany spends 15.6% of its budget on healthcare and achieves a life expectancy of 80.5 years with an infant mortality rate of 3.1 per 1,000 live births, while the US spends 25.9% of its budget and achieves a life expectancy of 78.4 years with an infant mortality rate of 5.5 per 1,000 live births. Germany provides universal coverage to 100% of its population while the US leaves 27 million people uninsured despite higher spending.

AUS.text: Australia achieves a life expectancy of 83.1 years and infant mortality of 3.1 per 1,000 live births while spending 19.0% of its budget on healthcare, compared to the US spending 25.9% of its budget to achieve a life expectancy of 78.4 years and infant mortality of 5.5 per 1,000 live births. Australia's universal healthcare system covers 100% of its population while the US system leaves 27 million people uninsured despite higher overall spending.

JPN.text: Japan achieves a life expectancy of 84.0 years and an infant mortality rate of 1.8 per 1,000 live births while spending 20.1% of its budget on healthcare, compared to the US spending 25.9% of its budget to achieve a life expectancy of 78.4 years and an infant mortality rate of 5.5 per 1,000 live births.

KOR.text: South Korea spends 14.8% of its budget on healthcare and achieves a life expectancy of 83.4 years with an infant mortality rate of 2.3 per 1,000 live births, while the US spends 25.9% of its budget and achieves a life expectancy of 78.4 years with an infant mortality rate of 5.5 per 1,000 live births. South Korea provides universal coverage to 100% of its population while the US covers approximately 92%, leaving 27 million uninsured.

FRA.text: France spends 15.5% of its budget on healthcare and achieves a life expectancy of 82.9 years with 3.4 infant deaths per 1,000 live births, while the US spends 25.9% of its budget and achieves 78.4 years life expectancy with 5.5 infant deaths per 1,000 live births. France covers 100% of its population while the US leaves 27 million people uninsured despite higher spending.

GBR.text: The United Kingdom allocates 11.5% of its budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet both countries spend roughly similar shares of GDP on education at 5.9% and 5.4% respectively, with comparable tertiary enrollment rates of 80.5% and 79.4%.

DEU.text: Germany allocates 11.3% of its budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet both countries spend nearly identical shares of GDP on education at 5.4% and 5.2% respectively, with comparable tertiary enrollment rates of 78.6% and 79.4%.

AUS.text: Australia allocates 16.4% of its budget to education and achieves 104.6% tertiary enrollment compared to the US at 79.4% enrollment while spending only 3.8% of its budget on education.

JPN.text: Japan allocates 9.0% of its national budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet the US spends 5.4% of GDP on education versus Japan's 3.3%, while achieving higher tertiary enrollment rates of 79.4% compared to Japan's 64.9%.

KOR.text: South Korea allocates 16.9% of its budget to education and achieves 106.7% tertiary enrollment, while the US spends 3.8% of its budget on education with 79.4% tertiary enrollment.

FRA.text: France allocates 11.4% of its national budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet both countries spend nearly identical shares of GDP on education at approximately 5.3-5.4%. The US achieves higher tertiary enrollment at 79.4% versus France's 70.5% despite dedicating less than one-third the budget share to education.

GBR.text: The United Kingdom spends 13.1% of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini index of 32.4 with the bottom 20% earning 7.7% of total income, while the United States spends 9.4% and has a Gini index of 41.8 with the bottom 20% earning 5.1% of total income.

DEU.text: Germany spends 16.2% of its budget on income security compared to the US at 9.4%, yet achieves a Gini coefficient of 32.4 versus the US at 41.8, with the bottom 20% earning 7.8% of income in Germany versus 5.1% in the US. The higher German spending correlates with measurably lower income inequality and greater income share for lower earners.

AUS.text: Australia spends 1.2 percentage points more of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini coefficient of 33.8 compared to the US at 41.8, with the bottom 20 percent earning 7.3 percent of income versus 5.1 percent in the US.

JPN.text: Japan spends 16.0% of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini index of 32.3, while the US spends 9.4% and has a Gini index of 41.8. The bottom 20% of earners in Japan capture 6.9% of income compared to 5.1% in the US.

KOR.text: South Korea spends 10.5% of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini index of 32.9, while the US spends 9.4% and has a Gini index of 41.8. The bottom 20% of earners in South Korea capture 7.5% of national income compared to 5.1% in the US.

FRA.text: France allocates 16.4% of its budget to income security compared to the US at 9.4%, yet achieves a Gini coefficient of 31.8 versus the US at 41.8, with the bottom 20% earning 7.7% of income in France versus 5.1% in the US. The higher French spending correlates with measurably lower income inequality and greater income share for lower earners.

GBR.text: The United Kingdom allocates 10.8% of its budget to infrastructure and achieves 41.44 broadband connections per 100 people, while the United States spends 2.5% of its budget and has 38.15 connections per 100 people.

DEU.text: Germany allocates 11.2% of its budget to infrastructure and achieves 45.38 broadband subscriptions per 100 people, while the US spends 2.5% of its budget and has 38.15 subscriptions per 100 people.

AUS.text: Australia allocates 12.7% of its budget to infrastructure compared to the US at 2.5%, yet both countries achieve similar broadband coverage at approximately 37 per 100 people.

JPN.text: Japan allocates 11.7% of its budget to infrastructure compared to 2.5% for the US, yet both countries achieve nearly identical broadband penetration at approximately 38.5 per 100 people.

KOR.text: South Korea allocates 14.6% of its budget to infrastructure and achieves 46.6 broadband subscriptions per 100 people, while the US spends 2.5% of its budget and has 38.1 subscriptions per 100 people.

FRA.text: France allocates 11.6% of its budget to infrastructure and achieves 48.67 broadband connections per 100 people, while the US spends 2.5% of its budget and has 38.15 connections per 100 people.

GBR.text: The United Kingdom spends 2.23% of its GDP on defense while achieving NATO membership and nuclear deterrence capabilities, compared to the US spending 3.30% of GDP on defense.

DEU.text: Germany spends 1.49% of GDP on defense while maintaining NATO membership and a functioning military, whereas the US spends 3.30% of GDP on defense—more than double Germany's ratio.

AUS.text: Australia spends 1.86% of GDP on defense while achieving the 12th largest military budget globally, compared to the US spending 3.30% of GDP for the world's largest military budget.

JPN.text: Japan spends 1.14% of GDP on defense while achieving the world's third-largest economy and maintaining regional security, compared to the US spending 3.30% of GDP on defense.

KOR.text: South Korea maintains military readiness while spending 2.6% of GDP on defense, compared to the US spending 3.3% of GDP despite facing a similar security environment on the Korean Peninsula.

FRA.text: France spends 1.95% of GDP on defense while maintaining NATO membership and a permanent UN Security Council seat, compared to the US spending 3.30% of GDP on defense.

GBR.text: The United Kingdom allocates 1.5% of its budget to science and R&D while spending 2.9% of GDP on research, compared to the US which allocates 1.1% of budget but spends 3.6% of GDP on research.

DEU.text: Germany allocates 1.2% of its budget to science and R&D while spending 3.13% of GDP on research, compared to the US which allocates 1.1% of budget but spends 3.59% of GDP on research.

AUS.text: Australia allocates 2.3% of its budget to science and R&D while spending 1.85% of GDP on research, compared to the US spending 1.1% of budget but 3.59% of GDP on research.

JPN.text: Japan allocates 2.8% of its budget to science and R&D compared to the US at 1.1%, yet both countries spend roughly equivalent shares of GDP on research at 3.4% and 3.6% respectively.

KOR.text: South Korea invests 5.21% of its GDP in research and development while spending 2.7% of its national budget on science and R&D, compared to the US investment of 3.59% of GDP and 1.1% of budget allocation.

FRA.text: France allocates 1.8% of its budget to science and R&D while spending 2.23% of GDP on research, producing a Nobel Prize winner every 5.2 million citizens. The US spends 1.1% of its budget despite investing 3.59% of GDP on R&D, yielding one Nobel Prize winner per 9.8 million citizens.
